description: Human hair is real hair collected from donors that is typically treated and processed for use inwigs, extensions, and braiding, explains Chisholm: "It is praised for its natural appearance, versatility, and longevity," she adds.

Human hair is used in various protective styles including knotlessboho braids, kinky twists, andmicro braidsbecause "it offers a natural look and feel and can blend seamlessly with natural hair," Kimble says.

Human hair requires more care than synthetic. It's prone to dryness, so it needs to be moisturized regularly with lightweight oils and leave-in conditioners. Additionally, human hair will need to be gently detangled. Chisholm suggests doing this with a wide-tooth comb or your fingers to avoid breakage, starting from the top to the end to ensure that any knots have been removed from the hair. Braids with human hair should be wrapped at night with asilk scarfor bonnet to reduce frizz.
Synthetic hair is made from artificial fibers designed to mimic the appearance of human hair. It's available in a variety of colors, textures, and lengths.

Synthetic hair is "often used for braided styles such asbox braidsorcornrows," explains Kimble. "It holds its shape well, which makes it ideal for creating consistent, pre-styled looks such as curly or straight braids." Other styles that commonly use synthetic hair include feed-in braids, knotless braids, faux locs or butterfly locs, and Senegalese or Marley twists.

Unlike human hair, synthetic hair requires minimal maintenance. You can and should wash it with a mild shampoo, but "synthetic hair shouldn't be washed excessively as this can cause it to lose its texture," Chisholm says. It can get frizzy after a while, but this can be managed by using mousse and hair spray together.

Human hair and synthetic hair are both great options for braids. If you're looking for hair that has a natural, lightweight feel and is longer lasting, human hair is a great option. If you're on a budget and seek cost-friendly bundles with a lot of styling flexibility, synthetic hair might be right for you. Ultimately, one hair type isn't better than the other—the final call will simply boil down to your needs and preferences.

Yes, you read that right: Each tool is priced under $50 and the two complimentary haircare products launching alongside them—a leave-in heat protectant and glowy hair oil—are each under $10. Of course, the brand's expansion into the haircare world keeps its buzzy, accessible price point while promising salon-worthy results (that's what the brand is known for after all). The new selection includes an automatic curler, blowout brush, curling iron, and straightener—essentially every tool you'd ever need to re-create all the hairstyles saved on your mood boards.What's more, each product is coated in the brand's Gloss Pro technology, which helps regulate temperature to avoid damaging strands, so you're left with healthy, hydrated hair between each style.

The It-Curl One-Touch Instant Curler is the most unique tool on the lineup. The tulip-shaped tool is an automatic curler that wraps the hair without snagging, creating bouncy curls in under 60 seconds. What's more, it has an outer shell to protect strands from the heat
This voluminous, blown-out look exudes "Old Hollywood" glamour. That's why a sophisticated black ribbon is the perfect accessory.
You cannot go wrong with a simple, classic bow. This look is perfect for curly or textured hair: Secure your hair in a curly-safepineappleand add your ribbon accent to elevate yourprotective style.
What's better than one braid?Two braids. Specifically, ones that are adorned with a trio of mismatched bows. Tie the ribbons around each hair tie, or, as seen above, clip on some bow barrettes.

Frida Kahlo—one of the original braid crown icons—was famously pictured with ribbons incorporated into her intricately braided updos. Morales credits Kahlo as an originator of the trend. "I am sure Frida Kahlo has to do something with it," she says. "She had fun with her beautiful hair and created this trademark over the years. I am almost sure this trend comes from her."
A propercrown braidcan be tough to master on your own, so an easy dupe is to form two separate braids and pin them to the crown of your head. You can emulate Frida by integrating a ribbon throughout your braids before pulling them into a crown.

We love the drama of this oversized bow, which offers a fresh take on the bow hairstyle. A monochrome and muted color palette (like this all-black take) will help keep the oversized style from looking costumey. Follow Kiernan Shipka's lead and pair this style with a dramatic winged eyeshadow look for maximum impact.

Why stop at just one ribbon? Multiple small ribbons are an easy way to incorporate the accessory throughout a hairstyle with ease. This quadruple ribbon look onHailey Bieberis a great way to accent standard braids with multiple mini bows.
Layering is the name of the game with this bow hairstyle. The two bows add such a dramatic, voluminous effect to a slicked-back bun.
For a flirty updo, try pairing a retro-curled high ponytail with strategically placed satin micro bows.

Take yourponytailto the next level with an elegant satin ribbon. Once your hair is up in a high ponytail, tie a piece of ribbon into a bow around the base. "I like to incorporate ribbons into the hair [with] braids, buns, and ponytails," Morales says. "I love the way it changes the look and makes it more fun."

One surefire way to add drama to an everyday hairstyle is an extra-long ribbon. A cascade of ribbon extending past the length of your hair is sure to turn heads and give off an almost Rapunzel vibe.
When selecting a ribbon for this style, longer is better—especially if you're creating a bow. We love Ariana's use of transparent ribbon in this look, which adds to the ethereal nature of the whole ensemble.

Ribbons in solid statement colors are a great complement to pattern-heavy looks. You can use this method to bring out more subtle colors in a busy design: The key is choosing one color from the pattern and playing off its tones. For example, this gorgeous gold ribbon brings out the neutral tones in the cheetah print dress and helps to brighten the whole look despite the darker colors being more prominent.

This now-iconic Met Gala look relied on a ribbon to raise a basic ponytail to red-carpet-ready glamour. Ribbon fabrics vary, so try using something with texture to add intrigue and definition to your look.

Finding a similarly patterned ribbon is a great way to tie your hair into your outfit. Here, a simple low ponytail is instantly elevated by a hair ribbon that coordinates with the striped pants. Keeping a neutral color, like a white top, in between will help you avoid looking too matchy-matchy.

Ribbon hairstyles can be further elevated when paired with other clips and accessories. In this look, the bow is smack dab in the middle of a sea of bejeweled barrettes. You can replicate this with any hair clips (or even brooches) you have on hand.

"There are so many ways you can incorporate a ribbon into your hair, but some of my favorite ways to do it are weaving it into a loose braid or tying it at the end of your braid," Kim says. "The fun part is choosing [from] the different materials you can use, from thick satin ribbons to printedscarves."

Pull back your bangs with a bow for a super easy style that still appears put-together. This hairstyle is also great for those growing out their hair and don't know what to do with an in-between length.
If a simple ribbon isn't delivering your desired effect, consider swapping in a bedazzled bow. We love how the sparkles on this dark-colored bow accentuate the center and create a starburst effect.

Short hair looks great when pulled back into a low pony at the nape of the neck—especially when you swap your elastic for a small, chic bow. The black bow beautifully complements the black coat and light hair in this look. Kim suggests beginners "start with a messier look so that imperfections are less noticeable and actually look good with them."
For a braided bun, start by braiding your hair. Once finished, wrap the braid into a bun and secure it in place with a bow. You could also incorporate a ribbon into the weaving of the braid.

With the rise inY2K hairstyles, why not combine the bow trend with one of the easiest early aughts styles to recreate, thebubble braid? Start by pulling your hair into a low ponytail, then use small pieces of ribbon to create the bubble braid sections. Feel free to alternate colors or even span the whole rainbow with your ribbon color choices.
Ahalf-up hairdois an effortless style that can take you from work to happy hour. Adding a black satin ribbon makes this half-up look even more chic.

Whether you're leaving your hair down or whipping up a quickmessy bun, a ribbon headband is a great way to make a last-minute hairstyle look more intentional. As a bonus, ribbon headbands may be more comfortable than traditional headbands, as you can customize how tightly they fit.

Why wear one bow when you can wear many? This maximalist approach to incorporating bows requires minimal effort to execute. To try this runway look at home, we recommend using mini clip-in bows and staggering them throughout the length of your hair.
Sabrina Carpenter is known for her chic hairstyles, and this is definitely one of our favorites. We love how she created a subtle half-up, half-down look with a sleek black ribbon.

"The glass hair trend refers to hair that is super smooth and shiny, almost to the point of being reflective like glass," says Fitzsimons. "Second day hair works well for a lot of styles, but you'll want to ensure your hair is freshly moisturized and has no build-up whatsoever."
First, shampoo and condition your hair. Fitzsimons recommends using shine-boosting products both in the shower and post-shower. "You can also use aleave-in conditionerlike theDaily Dose Miracle Moisture Leave-In Conditionerfor added shine and moisture," he says.

Next, applyheat protectionto your hair. (PSA: you should be doing this any time before you use heat styling tools.) You can find a list of our favoriteshere.
By the way... if you have super damaged hair from previous heat styling or chemical processes, Fitzsimons recommends avoiding this trend and focusing on nourishing your hair back to health first, (which will help it appear naturally shinier in the long-run).
"For most hair types, I would recommend a smooth blowout before using the flat iron to help minimize the amount of time you're using the flat iron on your hair," says Fitzsimons.

As Marjan explained to us while demoing the process IRL, taking ultra-fine sections (versus large chunks) and straightening them with a flat iron is the absolute key for nailing the glass hair look. For the record, all ofGHD's heat toolsare fabulous; it's the brand Marjan personally swears by.
To get a bit more specific with your technique, Marjan says to make sure to brush the hair on top of the iron, and then pull it down. Or, for an even smoother finish near the roots (and to flatten the hair at the top of the head), flip the brush. For the smoothest of smooth results, we (and virtually every hair expert and celebrity we talk to) swear byMason Pearson's luxe Popular Mixture Hair Brush. Yes, it's an investment. But rest assured it's a worthy one.

"Finish the look with an anti-frizz hairspray to ensure the style is locked all day long and use a few spritzes of ashine spray," advises Fitzsimons. You can spray directly on your hair, or spray your hairbrush with the hairspray and then brush through the ends for even distribution.
And that's it! Glass hair achieved.
Winner: Dyson Supersonic
Winner: L’Oreal Professionel AirLight Pro
Winner:  L’Oreal Professionel AirLight Pro

Ultimately, my favorite is the AirLight Pro because of its cutting-edge technology and personalization. As someone who tries to onlywash their hairtwice a week, I feel better knowing the infrared light and settings are working to minimize damage.

Amanda Rosenthalis a writer at Byrdie where she covers all things beauty. Her curiosity for new innovations fuels her love for makeup, skincare, and hair care. She is always interested in testing out the latest new products and deciphering whether big-ticket items are worth their price tag. She has fine, long hair, and her major hair concerns are to promote hair growth and minimize damage. For this story, Amanda tested both dryers for a week at a time. She is most interested in cutting down dry time for her longer hair while causing the least heat damage.

Thompson used all Living Proof products for the weekend, starting with the welcome dinner. For the occasion, Thompson and Condor decided on "effortlessly timeless, chic waves that showed of her long natural hair." The carefree style was the perfect pairing to her chic corseted gown and soft makeup. To give the curls a modern, easygoing feel, Thompson added a bit of texture to the waves, starting by applying thePerfect Hair Day Dry Shampooto the roots, followed by theFull Dry Volume & Texture Spraythroughout the hair for a touch of volume. The finished waves were topped with theStyleLab Flex Hairsprayto keep them in place all night long.

Hear those bells? On her big day Condor opted for "glamorous classic waves" that looked so pretty paired with her strapless gown and pearls. There was an emphasis on the style feeling "polished, smooth, and elegant," says Thompson, who wanted to "achieve soft, structured waves that frame her face."

To end the celebrations on a high note, Condor's farewell brunch look included a fun high ponytail with a sleek side part that could show off the details on the back of her dress. "We wanted the ponytail to have clean lines and volume at the base, giving it both structure and movement," says Thompson. The Smooth Styling Spray was a go-to for Thompson throughout the getting ready process, and she used it again on day three to create a silky smooth base before styling. Thompson swept Condor's hair back into a high ponytail at the crown, leaving the front section out to craft the side part. The look was complete with lots of shine and a touch of hairspray.

Our stylists agree: Pulled-up, artfully undone hair is great for French-girl vibes. "A tousledhalf-updois my idea of a sexy French cool girl look," says Streicher. "I love the effortless feel, but also the intentioned volume and sexiness of it." Haliti is also a fan, pointing out that "low-maintenance updos are also universally flattering [and] can be altered depending on personal preference."
The main thing to remember when copying this look: "It should feel "effortless and 'lived in,' but not too messy," says Streicher.

Frenchbalayageexploded in the 1970s, and it's been a popular (and chic) alternative to traditional highlights ever since. Characterized by a smudged root and subtle and seamless transitions, the hand-paintedhighlightsare lower-maintenance and require less upkeep. While the highly customized nature means this technique can work for any face shape, Haliti particularly likes it for round and oval, "as it adds depth and dimension."

Face-framinglayers are universally flattering and do exactly what the name says: frame your face. Sometimes referred to as a long angle cut, to get the look, the stylist will cut layers around the face—beginning at the chin or jawline—then blend them into the rest of the hair. The look works with all face shapes, though Haliti says it's especially great for ovals and hearts. To really make the style shine, she recommends styling your hair with ablow dryer and round brushto enhance the layers and make them pop.

Another universally flattering style, undone waves are "an old trend that remains popular due to its effortless appearance," says Haliti. She outlines the steps for getting the low-key-yet-sophisticated look for yourself:

Popular in the 1960s,curtain bangshave always managed to make a comeback over the years; sometimes as messy, feathered fringe, sometimes in a longer, more layered fashion,sometimes even as baby bangs. "They can be shorter microbangs or longer cheekbone-length fringe that adds a bit of softness to the front of any textured bob, lob, or layered hairstyle," says Streicher. Her tips to style them:

When many think ofbobs, they think of 1920s flappers sporting pin-straight, ear-skimming cuts. But as anyone who keeps tabs oncelebrity hair trendsknows, they've "resurfaced with a modern twist," as Haliti says. Today's bobs are more touseled, with many embracing loose waves and soft curls.
The cheekbone-lengthFrench bob"accentuates the jawline and cheekbones, so it's best for square, heart, and oval faces," says Haliti. For averyFrench feel, use a curling or flat iron to create waves, then add texture spray to create that undone, touseled look.

For a dressier moment, "I love taking a classic, like pearls, and dressing the hair down, creating a cool and edge look," Streicher tells us. She recommends a messyhalf-up French girl bun. "I love a half-updo that is basically a downdo, but with some intentional volume when you need it," she says. "This is great for anyone who needs some extra volume on top of the head." To achieve the look:

trend_name: Taylor Swift Paired Her Massive Chiefs Jersey With a Cute Hair Accessory
description:
CONFIDENCE, COMMUNITY, AND JOY
Bows forever.
Getty Images
I don't care what the NFL says, the official start of football season is whenTaylor Swiftmakes her first appearance at a Chiefs game. And, the time has finally come: Last week, Swift arrived to support Kelcewearing a denim two-piece setat the first game of the year, and for yesterday's game, she opted for an oversized Chiefs t-shirt with a cute hairstyle to match.
It goes without saying that Swift has perfected her signature glam (you can probably picture it perfectly in your head.) For Sunday's game against the Bengals,The Tortured Poets Departmentsinger went for her favorite outfit formula—an oversized Chiefs shirtdress and thigh-high boots—with a matchingred lip. To our surprise, she wrapped the outfit up with a bow (literally), donning a cute redhair bowwith her half-up half-down look.
Getty Images
Let's break down her hair look: To start, she left her iconic swooshy bangs alone and swept them ever so softly to the side with pieces sitting right at her brow bones. She left one longer face-framing piece out before pulling the other front pieces to the back of her hair and connecting them with a pretty red bow. The rest of her hair looked like she let it air dry naturally into soft waves.
The rest of her glam was so Swift-coded. Think: her signature fieryred lipstick, black winged eyeliner with wispy lashes, and soft mattecloud skin. She likes what she likes!
Getty Images
Has Swift worn a hair bow before? Probably, but not recently (or at least in recent memory). The bow trend has slowed down a little bit, but perhaps with Swift's co-sign the dainty accessory will be back. Only time will tell! And, until then, we'll be wearing our bows anyway.
